data.title: Investors hold their nerve on aerospace amid conflict risks: BNP Paribas
data.description:
Aerospace and defense companies are heading into first-quarter earnings with geopolitical tensions firmly in focus, but early signals suggest limited near-term disruption to commercial demand, analyst Matthew Akers of BNP Paribas said in a March 30 note to clients.

Recent conflict involving Iran has yet to meaningfully alter customer behavior for major manufacturers, including Boeing (BA) and GE Aerospace (GE), he said. For Boeing (BA), exposure to the Middle East accounts for only a low-to-mid teens percentage of its commercial aircraft backlog, with many deliveries scheduled for the next decade.

Recent production concerns, including a wiring issue affecting roughly two dozen aircraft, appear contained. The issue is expected to shift some 737 Max deliveries from the first quarter into the second without affecting full-year guidance.

On the defense side, however, heightened tensions could provide a tailwind. Boeing’s (BA) defense unit supplies munitions such as JDAM kits, Small Diameter Bombs and bunker busters, as well as components for missile defense systems, positioning it to benefit from increased demand.

GE Aerospace (GE) is seeing a similarly muted near-term impact. The company’s exposure to Middle Eastern fleets is less than 10%, and any slowdown in services revenue would likely take about a year to materialize. First-quarter cash flow is expected to decline year over year, while deliveries of its GE9X engines are anticipated to be more evenly distributed across the year.

Fuel price uncertainty

After meeting roughly 70 investors across Europe, Akers said the dominant sentiment is one of cautious stability. While rising fuel costs tied to geopolitical tensions remain a risk, airlines have yet to make significant changes to fleet plans, and reductions in flying activity appear manageable.

As a result, many investors are maintaining positions in aerospace stocks, viewing the sector as supported by long-term structural growth. However, expectations for near-term upside appear muted heading into earnings season.

At the same time, some investors expressed concern about valuations among U.S. aerospace companies, with a preference emerging for European peers that offer similar exposure at lower multiples.

Defense divergence between U.S., Europe

In defense, a valuation gap between U.S. and European companies continues to draw attention. European defense stocks are trading at a premium, reflecting increased regional spending, while U.S. firms are seen as offering more limited exposure to that trend.

Exports to Europe account for only about 10% of revenue for many U.S. defense contractors, tempering enthusiasm among European investors seeking direct beneficiaries of the region’s defense buildup.

Environmental, social and governance restrictions also remain a barrier. Some European funds are still unable to invest in U.S. defense names, although those limitations are gradually easing.

Companies such as L3Harris Technologies (LHX) and Howmet Aerospace (HWM) continue to attract investor interest, with Howmet in particular standing out for its consistent execution and balanced exposure between original equipment and aftermarket demand.

Long-term industry structure comes into focus

Beyond near-term earnings, investors are increasingly focused on the long-term structure of the aerospace industry. Questions are emerging around how value will be distributed in the next generation of aircraft, particularly as manufacturers consider bringing more production in-house.

Competition in the engine market is also intensifying. Ongoing disputes between major players have fueled speculation that new entrants or partnerships could reshape supplier dynamics for future narrowbody aircraft programs.

At the same time, original equipment manufacturers are expected to seek greater control over aftermarket services, a move that could improve margins but pressure suppliers.

Overall, while geopolitical risks and valuation debates persist, Akers’ meetings suggest investors remain broadly constructive on aerospace, even as they brace for a more measured tone from companies in the near term.

data.title: Identity, network and endpoint security stand least at risk to LLM disruption: Jefferies
data.description:
Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude Mythos leak demonstrated that frontier models continue to expand in capability, including cybersecurity, but Jefferies analysts contend that security vendors focused on identity, network, and endpoint security remain mostly insulated from potential AI disruption.

"We acknowledge that LLM advancements are not without any risk of cyber encroachment," said Jefferies analysts, led by Joseph Gallo, in an investor note. "That said, we see this as a likely advancement in capabilities into already perceived at-risk pockets of cyber (Threat Intel/Code Scanning/VM/SOAR/SOC) rather than expansion into other areas such as Identity, Network, and Endpoint (which we feel are more insulated, making the broad sell-off surprising). We continue to see the most impactful risks/debates as the potential for LLMs to detract incremental cyber budget spend from incumbents, although our prior survey work suggests that the budget reallocation risk is much more muted for cyber."

Some of the major endpoint, network, and identity protection providers include CrowdStrike (CRWD), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), Fortinet (FTNT), and SentinelOne (S).

Jefferies also pointed out that AI labs, such as Anthropic, are releasing some of their models early to security companies, indicating more of a partnership than a competitor.

"Anthropic's documents note the model is a precursor to a 'wave of models that can exploit vulnerabilities in ways that far outpace efforts of defenders,' and Anthropic appears to be pre-releasing to cyber vendors (to improve the robustness of their codebases), which we view as a sign of partnership rather than competition and positive for overall cyber demand," Gallo noted.

Several cybersecurity stocks were making gains during Monday market action. CrowdStrike was up 4.5%, SentinelOne had increased 2.7%, Palo Alto had jumped about 7%, and Fortinet had edged up 3%.

"While we understand the amplified uncertainty created by these headlines dictates a shoot first, ask questions later environment, we don't think it should be a surprise to see model improvements from Anthropic and other LLMs (this dynamic will continue)," Gallo added. "As the market takes a somewhat 'blanket' approach to diagnosing this risk, we believe there's an increasingly attractive opportunity in names exhibiting durable fundamentals, competitive AI positioning (we favor Identity as well as Network Security/Endpoint), and digestible valuations."

data.title: Wingstop, Shake Shack, and Dutch Bros are top restaurant picks at BNP Paribas
data.description:
BNP Paribas launched sweeping coverage on the restaurant sector. Analyst Steve McManus and his team favor high-quality concepts with strong unit economics and idiosyncratic growth drivers over those more reliant on the macro recovery or turnarounds. Wingstop (WING), Shake Shack (SHAK), and Dutch Bros (BROS) were named the top picks in the sector.

McManus views Wingstop (WING) as a long-duration, asset-light compounder with best-in-class franchise economics and a multi-year unit runway. The near-term comparable sales pressure for Wingstop (WING) is believed to be well understood and already priced into shares, which was noted to create an attractive entry point ahead of a re-acceleration in the second half of the year and 2027. 

The firm started off coverage on Wingstop with an Outperform rating and a price target of $275, based on a 30X EV/EBITDA multiple.

Shake Shack (SHAK) also landed an Outperform rating from BNP Paribas.

"From its origins as a hot dog cart in Madison Square Park, Shake Shack has become a global premium fast-casual brand known for its burgers and shakes. And after several years of uneven comp trends and cost volatility, Shake Shack looks to be entering a more durable phase of earnings growth. We see improving risk/reward as execution stabilizes and margins inflect," highlighted  McManus.

BNP Paribas is also bullish on Dutch Bros (BROS). The coffee chain's unique drive-thru beverage platform, offering everything from  “protein coffee” to energy drinks, shakes & smoothies, and seasonal drinks, was said to be anchored by a loyal, high-frequency customer base that skews Gen Z. "Within the fast-growing specialty beverage category, BROS is emerging as a durable share gainer, supported by strong unit economics and a long runway for unit expansion," highlighted McManis. 

The firm initiated coverage on Dutch Bros (BROS) with an Outperform rating and price target of $73.

data.title: Morgan Stanley's E*Trade in talks to lead SpaceX IPO for small investors - report
data.description:
Morgan Stanley's (MS) E*Trade is in talks to take the leading role for smaller U.S. investors in SpaceX's (SPACE) IPO. 

Robinhood (HOOD) and SoFi Technologies (SOF) have also pitched for the small retail role in the IPO, but SpaceX is considering cutting them both out, according to a  Reuters report on Monday, which cited people familiar with the matter. The two companies remain in talks to handle some of the sales.

Mutual fund Fidelity is also trying to see if it can distribute some of the SpaceX (SPAC) shares on its trading platform, according to the report. 

Robinhood, Morgan Stanley, SoFi and Fidelity declined to comment to Reuters. SpaceX did not respond to a request for comment.

The latest update comes after Reuters reported last Thursday that Elon Musk is talking about allocating as much as 30% of SpaceX's IPO to individual investors.

data.title: BP loses head of electric vehicle charging division for second time in a year - FT
data.description:
BP (BP) has lost the head of its electric vehicle charging division for the second time in a year, the Financial Times reported Monday, as the company scales back investment in its energy transition businesses ahead of the arrival of its new CEO this week.

Martin Thomsen, who took charge of BP Pulse (BP) as well as the company's gasoline station and retail business in Europe and South Africa last May, joined Rolls-Royce (RYCEY) (RYCEF) this month as chief procurement and supply chain officer, according to the report.

BP Pulse (BP), which lost former CEO Richard Bartlett in March a year ago, focuses on electric vehicle charging in the U.K., U.S., Germany, and China, and had targeted 100K public charging points by 2030.

Pulse sold more than 1.5 TWh of energy last year, an all-time high for the business, marking an 8% Y/Y increase, but BP (BP) is moving away from the clean energy businesses, and last year shut down the team responsible for developing electric, hydrogen, and other low-emission solutions for vehicles.

Thomsen's departure comes as former Woodside Energy head Meg O'Neill is set to take over as BP's (BP) CEO) on Wednesday, replacing Murray Auchincloss.

data.title: OpenAI's ChatGPT app store initiative sees sluggish start: report
data.description:
Six months since OpenAI's (OPENAI) initiative to integrate mini apps within ChatGPT was announced, the progress has been sluggish, Bloomberg News reported, citing interviews with app makers.

There are now over 300 app integrations available, but they are hidden away, and the functionality has been limited by the partner companies, which are hesitant to hand over customer relationships and payments to OpenAI. Developers have also complained regarding a tedious app-approval process, a buggy coding system, and a lack of usage data, the report added.

"Our apps platform is central to our product strategy. We’re still early in building this out, and we recognize there are areas where the developer experience needs to improve. We’re committed to working closely with partners and developers to make the platform more reliable, more predictable, and easier to build on over time. We’re optimistic about the future of the ecosystem and the opportunities ahead for developers building in ChatGPT," said OpenAI in an emailed statement to Seeking Alpha.

The ChatGPT app push has the potential to escalate increasing competition with Apple (AAPL), steering consumers toward a new ecosystem for apps and services outside the App Store. Apple moved to rein that in November 2025, introducing a “mini apps” policy requiring so-called super apps to hand over a 15% cut of in-app purchases, the report noted.

StubHub and Booking (BKNG) do not yet see ChatGPT apps as a big marketing driver. "It’s easier to discover listings on Booking.com,” Booking CEO Glenn Fogel said in an interview, noting that referral traffic from ChatGPT is still "small." He added that the company still “spends an enormous amount” on advertising with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) relative to what it spends on OpenAI’s chatbot.

Several initial partners, including Figma (FIG), Expedia (EXPE) and Target (TGT), saw their stocks soar after the initial announcement, as the tie-up offered a new way to acquire users in the age of AI rather than be disrupted by it, according to the report.

DoorDash (DASH) has also seen that no single collaboration integration, whether it is the order button on ChatGPT, Google, or Yelp, "monopolizes customer attention," as per a company spokesperson, the report noted.

Few apps offer a checkout option that doesn’t force users to leave OpenAI’s chatbot. In the case of Uber, users must begin the experience with "@Uber" before telling the chatbot their pickup and dropoff points to see an estimated fare range. Riders can only complete the booking on Uber, in which the whole process is faster, the report added.

Ticket reseller StubHub allows ChatGPT users to search for concerts, events, and seat availability based on preferences such as budget or viewing angles. However, it also requires them to visit the website to complete their purchase or even just to zoom in on a seating map.

StubHub President and Chief Product Officer Nayaab Islam said that customers are not yet using chatbots as a “primary way” to buy things and cited consumer apprehension about sharing credit card details with AI assistants, the report added.

However, he noted that it would be "imprudent" to not participate in chatbot apps given the billions of users they have.

Developers have also found the app development process frustrating, the report noted.

Hanh Nguyen, CEO of Fractal, a startup that makes tools for non-coders to build chatbot apps, said that getting an app published to the app store has been a lengthy ordeal for some developers. She said some rejections have included "false signals" flagged by OpenAI's AI-assisted review system, which required intervention from the company’s human support staff to resolve, the report added.

OpenAI said it has been accelerating the pace on approvals, citing both a streamlined process and developers addressing previously identified issues, according to the report.

Elliot Garreffa, a co-founder of a third-party platform to track ChatGPT apps and test their performance, said some developers run into problems even after their apps are accepted. Because OpenAI defines chatbot prompts as private data, the programmers have found they receive “very limited” analytics on their app's performance, leaving them "running quite blind" regarding user engagement, Garreffa added.

Update: The story was updated with a statement from OpenAI.

data.title: Moleculin Biotech files to sell 6.37M shares of common stock for holders
data.description:
• Moleculin Biotech (NASDAQ:MBRX) filed to sell 6.37M shares of common stock.
  • This prospectus is not an offer to sell these securities.
  • The company will not receive any proceeds from the sale of common stocks by the selling shareholder.
  • MBRX down by 6.56%.
  • Filing.

data.title: United Natural Foods is the best performing consumer staples stock in March
data.description:
The S&P 500 Consumer Staples sector dropped about 8% through March as the market remained volatile amid economic and geopolitical concerns.

Below is a list of the top 10 consumer staples stocks based on their March performance. The list includes companies from various industries within the consumer staples sector, such as food distributors, packaged foods and meats, agricultural products and services, and food retail. 

The list is topped by United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI), with an impressive one-month performance of 20.20% and a Strong Buy Quant Rating of 4.97. The Andersons, Inc. (ANDE) and Darling Ingredients Inc. (DAR) follow closely behind, both also carrying Strong Buy ratings of 4.63 and 4.75 respectively.

Other notable performers rounding out the top ten include Seaboard Corporation (SEB), The Kroger Co. (KR), and Smithfield Foods, Inc. (SFD). The list features a range of Quant Ratings, from the Strong Buy rating of Smithfield Foods, Inc. (SFD) at 4.78 to the Sell rating of Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM) at 1.69.

Here is the list:

  1. United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI), 1 month performance percentage: 20.20%

  2. The Andersons, Inc. (ANDE), 1 month performance percentage: 12.10%

  3. Darling Ingredients Inc. (DAR), 1 month performance percentage: 11.51%

  4. Seaboard Corporation (SEB), 1 month performance percentage: 8.22%

  5. The Kroger Co. (KR), 1 month performance percentage: 7.30%

  6. Smithfield Foods, Inc. (SFD), 1 month performance percentage: 7.27%

  7. Bunge Global SA (BG), 1 month performance percentage: 6.69%

  8. Target Corporation (TGT), 1 month performance percentage: 5.32%

  9. Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM), 1 month performance percentage: 4.78%

  10. Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM), 1 month performance percentage: 4.62%

Consumer Staples ETFs: (XLP), (VDC), (IYK), (FSTA), (KXI), and (RSPS)

data.title: Applied Materials, Lam Research stay BofA's top semicap picks as spending estimates rise
data.description:
Applied Materials (AMAT) and Lam Research (LRCX) are still Bank of America's top picks in the semiconductor equipment space, as the investment firm now sees equipment spending continuing to rise.

“We update our [wafer fab equipment] estimates following an eventful earnings season where broadening AI wafer demand led to all major semicaps calling out an unusually robust environment (up to 12-24 month visibility) where impressive CY26 guides for LRCX (>23% YoY sales growth), KLAC (high-teens YoY sales growth), and AMAT (+20% YoY Systems growth) accelerate CY27,” analyst Vivek Arya wrote in a note to clients. Arya now sees chip equipment spending at $140B in 2026, $171B in 2027, and $193B in 2028, compared to prior estimates of $131B, $150B, and $155B, respectively. He also expects 2029 and 2030 spending levels to hit $186B and $201B.

“We think this industry expansion, growing 18% CY25-28E CAGR, might still be a supply-constrained view but supports semi sales growing from ~$780bn CY25 to almost $1.3tn by CY28E (17% CAGR), reflecting healthy mid-teens % intensity. Agentic AI crowds out leading-edge; new capacity needed. As XPU/accelerator roadmaps progress and as agentic AI takes off, scarce capacity for leading-edge logic becomes highly competitive,” Arya added.

3 nanometer push

Delving deeper, Arya said there is crowding in the 3-nanometer space, as Nvidia's (NVDA) Rubin competes with AMD's (AMD) MI350X and MI400X, Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) TPU v7, and Amazon's (AMZN) Trainium 3. There's also a bottleneck in CPUs, as Intel (INTC) and AMD raise prices and Nvidia's Vera CPU drives “further tightness,” Arya explained.

Switches have also seen scaling at the 3-nanometer level, with Nvidia's NVLink 6 and Marvell (MRVL) and Broadcom (AVGO) increasing outputs. 

“The broadening of AI chips for rack-scale architecture (Vera Rubin POD uses 7 distinct chips), scaling of inference/agents, and design start diversity drives wafer demand well beyond current supply (UTRs ~100%),” Arya posited. “[Taiwan Semiconductor] (TSM) (pulling in fab timelines into CY27) and INTC/Samsung are racing to catch up. Separately, while too early to price in Elon Musk’s Terafab, we think initial output of 100K wspm could generate up to $20bn+ WFE [long-term].”

Memory rising!

Chip equipment makers are likely to also benefit from the continued demand for memory, as high-bandwidth memory and dynamic random access memory needs for AI continue to increase, Arya said. 

Further adding to the urgency is that Micron's (MU) capital spending, which is rising 25% year-over-year, is acquiring fabs to bring capacity online.

“While pricing could be stabilizing, long-term supply contracts (3-5 years) offer robust visibility and give memory suppliers confidence in cash flows to durably invest,” Arya added. “The ability to differentiate and charge premium pricing for HBM but also increasingly commodity DRAM is also an incentive for suppliers to invest. NAND upgrades to 300L also going strong into CY27.”

data.title: Powell says he's optimistic about job market, despite AI concerns
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell told students at Harvard University that he was optimistic about their long-term employment prospects, despite the proliferation of AI.

“There’s no denying it’s a challenging time to enter the labor market, but it may take some patience and all that, but in the longer term, this economy is going to give you great opportunities,” Powell said during a talk with students on Monday, according to Bloomberg.

Powell said that while the U.S. economy was undergoing structural changes due to AI and other advances, it remained dynamic.

Bloomberg noted the unemployment rate for 20- to 24-year-olds was 7.4% after hitting over 9% last year.
